content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN).

A simple path between two different vertices aaa and bbb (a<ba \lt ba<b) is called a clamp path if it does not contain any vertices with numbers smaller then aaa or greater than bbb.

Calculate the number of clamp paths in the given tree.
constraints: The given graph is a tree.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 2000) – the number of vertices of the tree. The second line contains N space‑separated integers p1 … pN. For each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N) if pi ≠ 0 there is an undirected edge between vertices i and pi. Exactly one pi equals 0 and denotes the root of the tree.
content.outputFormat: A single integer – the number of clamp paths in the given tree.

solution.approach: For a pair of vertices a < b the unique path between them is a clamp path iff the smallest vertex number on the path equals a and the largest equals b.  We preprocess the tree for LCA queries using binary lifting.  For every 2^k ancestor we also store the minimum and maximum vertex number on the upward segment.  Then for each ordered pair (a,b) we obtain the LCA, combine the minima and maxima on the two upward segments (a → LCA and b → LCA) and check whether min == a and max == b.  All such pairs are counted.  The preprocessing takes O(N log N) time and O(N log N) memory.  The double loop over all pairs costs O(N^2 log N) which is fast enough for N ≤ 2000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N + N^2 log N) – preprocessing O(N log N), each of the O(N^2) pairs needs O(log N) to obtain min/max on the path.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N log N) – binary lifting tables for parent, min and max.
